questions.question: What is the difference between val and var?
questions.title: What is the difference between val and var?

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: In Kotlin, val and var are used to declare variables, but they differ in mutability.

sections.heading: val (Immutable)
items: The value cannot be changed once assigned.
Helps write safer, predictable code.
Example:

sections.heading: val (Immutable)
sections.code:
val name = "Aasim"
      // name = "John" // ❌ Error

sections.heading: var (Mutable)
items: The value can be reassigned anytime.
Useful when the variable needs to change during program execution.
Example:

sections.heading: var (Mutable)
sections.code:
var age = 25
      age = 26 // ✅ Allowed

questions.question: What is the advantage of using const in Kotlin?
questions.title: What is the advantage of using const in Kotlin?

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: In Kotlin, const is used to declare compile-time constants. These are immutable values known at compile time, offering several advantages:

sections.heading: Advantages
items: Performance – Since values are determined at compile time, accessing them is faster than regular val.
Immutability – Ensures the value cannot be changed anywhere in the code.
Global Access – Can be used at the top level or in object declarations, making them accessible without creating an instance.
Cleaner Code – Ideal for defining constants like URLs, keys, or fixed numbers.

sections.heading: Example
sections.code:
const val BASE_URL = "https://api.example.com/"
const val MAX_RETRY = 3

questions.question: When to use lateinit and how to check if it has been initialized?
questions.title: When to use lateinit and how to check if it has been initialized?

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: lateinit is used to declare non-null variables without initializing them immediately. It is commonly used for dependencies or properties that will be initialized later, like Android views or injected objects.

sections.heading: When to Use lateinit
items: For non-null var properties that cannot be initialized in the constructor.
Common in Android for views:

sections.heading: Example
sections.code: lateinit var textView: TextView

sections.heading: How to Check Initialization
items: Use ::variableName.isInitialized to verify before accessing it:

questions.question: How to do lazy initialization of variables in Kotlin?
questions.title: How to do lazy initialization of variables in Kotlin?

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: Lazy initialization allows you to initialize a variable only when it is accessed for the first time, improving performance and saving resources.

sections.heading: How to Use lazy
items: Use the lazy delegate for read-only (val) properties.
The block runs only once, on the first access of name.

sections.heading: Key Points
items: lazy is thread-safe by default.
Ideal for expensive operations or values not needed immediately.
Only works with val, not var.

questions.question: What is an init block in Kotlin?
questions.title: What is an init block in Kotlin?

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: The init block in Kotlin is an initializer block that runs when an instance of a class is created. It is used to execute code or initialize properties during object creation.

sections.heading: Key Points
items: Can have multiple init blocks; they execute in the order defined.
Runs after primary constructor parameters are passed.
Often used for validation, logging, or complex property initialization.

sections.heading: Example

questions.question: Explain the difference between == and === in Kotlin.
questions.title: Explain the difference between == and === in Kotlin.

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: In Kotlin, == and === are used for comparison, but they check different things.

sections.heading: == (Structural Equality)
items: Checks if values are equal.
Calls the equals() method internally.
Example:

sections.heading: == (Structural Equality)
sections.code:
val a = "Hello"
val b = "Hello"
println(a == b) // ✅ true

sections.heading: === (Referential Equality)
items: Checks if both references point to the same object in memory.
Example:

sections.heading: === (Referential Equality)
sections.code:
val a = "Hello"
val b = "Hello"
println(a === b) // ✅ true or false depending on memory reference

questions.question: What are companion objects in Kotlin?
questions.title: What are companion objects in Kotlin?

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: A companion object is a singleton inside a class that holds class-level members, similar to Java's static.

sections.heading: Key Points
items: Declared with companion object inside a class.
Can contain constants, factory methods, utilities.
Accessed without creating an instance.

sections.heading: Example

questions.question: What is the equivalent of Java static methods in Kotlin?
questions.title: What is the equivalent of Java static methods in Kotlin?

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: Kotlin doesn't have static methods. You can achieve similar functionality using:

sections.heading: Companion Object
items: Class-level functions accessed without an instance:
Top-Level Functions – Functions outside a class, accessible directly:

sections.heading: Example
sections.code:
fun greet() = println("Hello")
greet()

questions.question: How to create a Singleton class in Kotlin?
questions.title: How to create a Singleton class in Kotlin?

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: In Kotlin, creating a singleton is simple using the object keyword. This ensures only one instance of the class exists.

sections.heading: Key Points
items: object creates a thread-safe singleton automatically.
No need for private constructor or getInstance() like in Java.
Can contain properties, methods, and initialization code.

questions.question: Explain open, abstract, final, internal, public, and private keywords in Kotlin.
questions.title: Explain open, abstract, final, internal, public, and private keywords in Kotlin.

sections.heading: Introduction
items: open – Class or function can be inherited or overridden.
abstract – Class or function must be implemented in a subclass; cannot be instantiated.
final – Default modifier; cannot be inherited or overridden.
public – Default visibility; accessible from anywhere.
private – Accessible within the same class only.
internal – Accessible within the same module.

questions.question: What is a data class in Kotlin and what methods are auto-generated?
questions.title: What is a data class in Kotlin and what methods are auto-generated?

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: A data class in Kotlin is a class designed to hold data. It automatically generates useful methods, reducing boilerplate code.

sections.heading: Auto-Generated Methods
items: equals() – Checks equality based on property values.
hashCode() – Generates hash code based on properties.
toString() – Returns a string representation of the object.
copy() – Creates a copy of the object with optional property changes.
componentN() – Functions for destructuring declarations.

sections.heading: Example
sections.code:
data class User(val name: String, val age: Int)

val user1 = User("Aasim", 25)
val user2 = user1.copy(age = 26)
println(user1) // Output: User(name=Aasim, age=25)

questions.question: Explain inline classes (value classes) in Kotlin.
questions.title: Explain inline classes (value classes) in Kotlin.

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: Inline classes (value classes) wrap a single value without creating an extra object at runtime, improving performance.

sections.heading: Key Points
items: Declared with @JvmInline value class.
Can have one property and methods.
Implements interfaces if needed.

sections.heading: Example
sections.content: `kotlin @JvmInline value class UserId(val id: String) fun printId(userId: UserId) { println(userId.id) } printId(UserId("123")) // Output: 123

questions.question: What is the purpose of inline, noinline, crossinline, and reified keywords?
questions.title: What is the purpose of inline, noinline, crossinline, and reified keywords?

sections.heading: inline, noinline, crossinline, and reified in Kotlin
items: inline – Inlines the function at the call site to reduce lambda overhead.
noinline – Prevents a lambda parameter of an inline function from being inlined.
crossinline – Prevents non-local returns in a lambda passed to an inline function.
reified – Allows access to a generic type at runtime in an inline function.

sections.heading: Example

questions.question: What are higher-order functions and how do you return a function from a function?
questions.title: What are higher-order functions and how do you return a function from a function?

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: A higher-order function takes a function as a parameter or returns a function.
sections.code:
fun operate(a: Int, b: Int, op: (Int, Int) -> Int) = op(a, b)
val sum = operate(5, 3) { x, y -> x + y } // 8

sections.heading: Example: Returning a Function

questions.question: What are lambda expressions in Kotlin?
questions.title: What are lambda expressions in Kotlin?

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: A lambda expression is an anonymous function that can be assigned, passed, or returned.

questions.question: What are extension functions and how do you use them?
questions.title: What are extension functions and how do you use them?

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: Extension functions allow you to add new functions to existing classes without inheriting or modifying them.

sections.heading: Key Points
items: Can be called like regular methods on the class.
Does not actually modify the original class.
Useful for enhancing library or built-in classes safely.

questions.question: Explain scope functions (let, apply, run, with, also) and their use-cases.
questions.title: Explain scope functions (let, apply, run, with, also) and their use-cases.

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: Scope functions provide a temporary scope for an object, improving readability and conciseness.

sections.heading: let – it as context; good for null checks or chaining.
sections.code: name?.let { println(it.length) }

sections.heading: run – this as context; returns lambda result; for computations.
sections.code: val result = "Hello".run { length + 5 }

sections.heading: with – this as context; called on object; for grouping operations.
sections.code: with(builder) { append("Hello"); append("World") }

sections.heading: apply – this as context; returns object; for object initialization.
sections.code: val person = Person().apply { name="Aasim"; age=25 }

sections.heading: also – it as context; returns object; for side-effects.
sections.code: val numbers = mutableListOf(1,2,3).also { println(it) }

questions.question: Difference between apply and with, and when to use each.
questions.title: Difference between apply and with, and when to use each.

sections.heading: apply
items: Called on an object, returns the object.
Use for object initialization.

sections.code: val person = Person().apply { name="Aasim"; age=25 }

sections.heading: with
items: Takes an object as parameter, returns lambda result.
Use for grouping operations or computing values.

sections.code: val length = with("Hello") { println(this); length }

questions.question: What are labels in Kotlin and how do you use them?
questions.title: What are labels in Kotlin and how do you use them?

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: Labels let you control flow in nested loops or lambdas using break, continue, or return.

sections.heading: Example
items: break@label – exit a specific loop
continue@label – continue a specific loop
return@label – return from a lambda or function

questions.question: Explain sealed classes and common use-cases in Android.
questions.title: Explain sealed classes and common use-cases in Android.

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: Sealed classes represent restricted class hierarchies where all possible subclasses are known at compile time.

sections.heading: Key Points
items: Declared using sealed class.
All subclasses must be defined in the same file.
Useful with when expressions because it ensures exhaustive checks (no else needed).

sections.heading: Common Android Use-Cases
items: UI State Handling – Loading, Success, Error states.
API Response Wrappers – Success, Failure, Loading results.
Navigation Events – Different navigation actions.
ViewModel State Management – Represent screen states clearly.

sections.heading: Example

questions.question: Difference between List and Array types in Kotlin.
questions.title: Difference between List and Array types in Kotlin.

sections.heading: List
items: Part of Kotlin Collections.
Usually immutable (listOf()).
Dynamic size.
Provides many built-in utility functions.

sections.heading: Array
items: Fixed size once created.
Mutable (elements can be changed).
Created using arrayOf().
Closer to low-level memory structure.

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: Use List → When you need flexible, collection-based operations. Use Array → When size is fixed or performance/memory control is needed.

questions.question: How to remove duplicates from an array or collection in Kotlin?
questions.title: How to remove duplicates from an array or collection in Kotlin?

sections.heading: distinct()
items: Use distinct() → Returns list with unique elements.

sections.code:
val list = listOf(1, 2, 2, 3, 3)
val unique = list.distinct()

sections.heading: toSet()
items: Use toSet() → Removes duplicates by converting to Set.

sections.code: val unique = list.toSet()

sections.heading: Example
items: For Array

sections.code:
val arr = arrayOf(1, 2, 2, 3)
val unique = arr.distinct()

sections.content: distinct() keeps order toSet() removes duplicates but may not guarantee order

questions.question: Explain Delegates (lazy, observable, vetoable) in Kotlin.
questions.title: Explain Delegates (lazy, observable, vetoable) in Kotlin.

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: Delegates allow you to delegate property behavior to another object.

sections.heading: lazy
items: Initializes value only when accessed first time.
Useful for expensive object creation.

sections.code: val data by lazy { loadData() }

sections.heading: observable
items: Triggers callback after value changes.
Useful for tracking or updating UI.

sections.heading: vetoable
items: Allows validation before value changes.
Change happens only if condition returns true.

questions.question: What are coroutines in Kotlin?
questions.title: What are coroutines in Kotlin?

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: Coroutines are a lightweight concurrency feature in Kotlin used to perform asynchronous and non-blocking tasks in a simple and efficient way.

sections.heading: Key Points
items: Help run background tasks like API calls, database operations, etc.
More efficient than traditional threads.
Use suspend functions to pause and resume execution without blocking the main thread.
Work with CoroutineScope, Dispatchers, and Jobs for lifecycle and threading control.

sections.heading: Example

questions.question: Difference between suspending and blocking functions.
questions.title: Difference between suspending and blocking functions.

sections.heading: Suspending Functions
items: Use the suspend keyword.
Pause execution without blocking the thread.
Allow other tasks to run while waiting (efficient).
Mainly used with coroutines.

sections.heading: Example
Blocking Functions
items: Block the current thread until the task finishes.
Prevent other tasks from running on that thread.
Can cause UI freezes if used on the main thread.

sections.heading: Example

questions.question: What is an inline function in Kotlin?
questions.title: What is an inline function in Kotlin?

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: An inline function in Kotlin is a function whose body is copied directly at the call site during compilation, instead of making a regular function call. This eliminates the overhead of lambda object creation and virtual calls.

sections.heading: Key Points
items: Declared using the inline keyword before fun.
Eliminates lambda allocation overhead — no Function object is created.
Best used for higher-order functions that accept lambdas.
Allows non-local returns from lambdas.
Should not be used for functions that don't accept lambdas — no benefit.

sections.heading: Example
When NOT to use inline
items: Functions with no lambda parameters — no performance gain.
Very large functions — inlining increases bytecode size.
Recursive functions — inline is not allowed.

questions.question: What is a reified keyword in Kotlin?
questions.title: What is a reified keyword in Kotlin?

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: reified is a keyword used with inline functions to retain the generic type information at runtime. Normally, Kotlin generics are erased at runtime (type erasure), but reified bypasses this limitation.

sections.heading: Key Points
items: Can only be used with inline functions.
Allows you to use T::class, is T, and as T inside the function.
Eliminates the need to pass Class<T> as a parameter.
Common in Android for Intent navigation, JSON parsing, etc.

sections.heading: Without reified (the old way)
With reified

questions.question: Suspending vs blocking in Kotlin Coroutines
questions.title: Suspending vs blocking in Kotlin Coroutines

sections.heading: Suspending
items: Uses the suspend keyword.
Pauses coroutine execution without blocking the underlying thread.
Thread is free to do other work while waiting.
Used with delay(), await(), withContext(), etc.

sections.heading: Suspending Example
Blocking
items: Blocks the current thread entirely until the task completes.
No other work can run on that thread during the wait.
Used with Thread.sleep(), blocking I/O, or runBlocking {}.

sections.heading: Blocking Example
Key Difference
sections.content: Suspending is coroutine-friendly and efficient. Blocking wastes thread resources and can cause ANR on Android if done on the main thread.

questions.question: Retrofit vs OkHttp — when to use each?
questions.title: Retrofit vs OkHttp — when to use each?

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: Both are networking libraries by Square, but they serve different purposes. OkHttp is the low-level HTTP client, while Retrofit is a high-level REST API client built on top of OkHttp.

sections.heading: OkHttp
items: Low-level HTTP client for making raw network requests.
Gives full control over requests, responses, caching, and interceptors.
Best when you need custom HTTP logic or non-REST communication.
More boilerplate code required.

sections.heading: Retrofit
items: High-level REST client built on OkHttp.
Converts API endpoints to Kotlin interfaces automatically.
Supports Coroutines, RxJava, Gson, Moshi out of the box.
Less boilerplate — ideal for standard REST API calls.

sections.heading: Retrofit Example
Summary
sections.content: Use Retrofit for REST APIs. Use OkHttp directly for custom networking scenarios or to add interceptors (logging, auth headers) to your Retrofit client.

questions.question: Launch vs Async in Kotlin Coroutines
questions.title: Launch vs Async in Kotlin Coroutines

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: Both launch and async are coroutine builders, but they differ in whether they return a result.

sections.heading: launch
items: Returns a Job — no result.
Fire-and-forget: used for tasks that don't return a value.
Exceptions propagate immediately to the parent scope.

sections.heading: launch Example
async
items: Returns a Deferred<T> — holds a future result.
Use await() to retrieve the result.
Exceptions are deferred until await() is called.
Ideal for parallel tasks that each return a value.

sections.heading: async Example
Parallel calls with async

questions.question: internal visibility modifier in Kotlin
questions.title: internal visibility modifier in Kotlin

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: The internal modifier in Kotlin restricts visibility to within the same module. A module is a set of Kotlin files compiled together — such as a Gradle module or an IntelliJ module.

sections.heading: Key Points
items: Accessible from anywhere within the same module.
Not accessible from other modules, even if they depend on yours.
Useful for hiding implementation details from external consumers.
More restrictive than public, less restrictive than private.

sections.heading: Example

questions.question: open keyword in Kotlin — what is it for?
questions.title: open keyword in Kotlin — what is it for?

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: In Kotlin, all classes and functions are final by default — they cannot be inherited or overridden. The open keyword explicitly allows a class or function to be extended or overridden.

sections.heading: Key Points
items: open on a class — allows other classes to inherit from it.
open on a function — allows subclasses to override it.
Without open, attempting to inherit or override causes a compile error.
abstract classes are implicitly open.

sections.heading: Example

questions.question: lateinit vs lazy in Kotlin
questions.title: lateinit vs lazy in Kotlin

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: Both lateinit and lazy are used to defer initialization, but they serve different use-cases and have different constraints.

sections.heading: lateinit
items: Used with var (mutable).
Can be initialized at any time before use.
Works only with non-nullable reference types (not Int, Boolean, etc.).
Throws UninitializedPropertyAccessException if accessed before init.
Common in Android for views, injected dependencies.

sections.heading: lateinit Example
sections.code:
lateinit var name: String
name = "Aasim" // Initialize later
println(name)

sections.heading: lazy
items: Used with val (immutable).
Initialized only once, on first access.
Thread-safe by default.
Works with any type including primitives.
Good for expensive computations or objects not always needed.

sections.heading: lazy Example
Quick Comparison
items: lateinit → var, initialized externally, reference types only.
lazy → val, initialized on first use, any type, thread-safe.

questions.question: How does Kotlin Multiplatform work?
questions.title: How does Kotlin Multiplatform work?

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: Kotlin Multiplatform (KMP) allows you to share business logic across multiple platforms — Android, iOS, Web, Desktop — while keeping platform-specific code separate.

sections.heading: How it Works
items: Common code — written in commonMain, shared across all platforms.
Platform-specific code — written in androidMain, iosMain, etc.
expect/actual mechanism — define an expected API in common, provide actual implementations per platform.
Kotlin compiles to JVM bytecode (Android/JVM), Native (iOS/macOS), and JavaScript (Web).

sections.heading: expect/actual Example
What can be shared
items: Business logic, data models, repositories.
Network calls (using Ktor), database (using SQLDelight).
ViewModels (using KMP-compatible architecture).

questions.question: What is a ViewModel and how is it useful?
questions.title: What is a ViewModel and how is it useful?

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: ViewModel is an Android Architecture Component designed to store and manage UI-related data in a lifecycle-aware manner. It survives configuration changes like screen rotation.

sections.heading: Why it is Useful
items: Survives configuration changes (rotation, theme change) — data is not lost.
Separates UI logic from Activity/Fragment — cleaner architecture.
Works with LiveData and StateFlow to reactively update the UI.
Scoped to the Activity or Fragment lifecycle — auto-cleared when not needed.
Supports viewModelScope for coroutine lifecycle management.

sections.heading: Example

questions.question: What is a JvmStatic annotation in Kotlin?
questions.title: What is a JvmStatic annotation in Kotlin?

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: @JvmStatic is an annotation that instructs the Kotlin compiler to generate an actual static method in the bytecode for a companion object or object function. Without it, Java callers must use a longer syntax.

sections.heading: Without @JvmStatic (Java call is verbose)
With @JvmStatic (Java call is clean)
Key Points
items: Used inside companion object or object declarations.
Makes the function callable as a true static from Java.
No effect when called from Kotlin — Kotlin handles it automatically.

questions.question: What is a JvmField annotation in Kotlin?
questions.title: What is a JvmField annotation in Kotlin?

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: @JvmField tells the Kotlin compiler to expose a Kotlin property as a plain Java field — without generating getter/setter methods. This improves Java interoperability and is useful for constants.

sections.heading: Without @JvmField
With @JvmField
Key Points
items: Removes getter/setter generation for the annotated property.
Commonly used with companion object constants for Java access.
Cannot be used with private properties or delegated properties.

questions.question: singleTask launchMode in Android
questions.title: singleTask launchMode in Android

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: singleTask is an Android Activity launch mode that ensures only one instance of the Activity exists in the back stack at any time. If the Activity already exists, the system brings it to the front and calls onNewIntent().

sections.heading: Behavior
items: Only one instance ever exists in the task.
If already in the stack, all Activities above it are destroyed (popped).
onNewIntent() is called with the new Intent instead of onCreate().
Activity runs in its own task by default (can be controlled with taskAffinity).

sections.heading: How to set in AndroidManifest.xml
sections.code:
<activity
    android:name=".MainActivity"
    android:launchMode="singleTask" />

sections.heading: Common Use Cases
items: Home/Dashboard screens — you don't want multiple instances.
Deep link handling — navigate to root without duplicating it.
Login screens — prevent going back to a duplicate login after auth.

questions.question: What is a JvmOverloads annotation in Kotlin?
questions.title: What is a JvmOverloads annotation in Kotlin?

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: @JvmOverloads instructs the Kotlin compiler to generate multiple overloaded versions of a function for Java — one for each parameter that has a default value. Java does not support default parameters natively.

sections.heading: Without @JvmOverloads
With @JvmOverloads
Key Points
items: Commonly used in custom Android Views with constructor overloads.
Generates N overloads for N default parameters.
No effect in Kotlin — Kotlin already handles default params.

questions.question: What is noinline in Kotlin?
questions.title: What is noinline in Kotlin?

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: noinline is used to mark specific lambda parameters of an inline function so they are NOT inlined. When a function is inline, all its lambda parameters are inlined by default. noinline opts specific ones out.

sections.heading: Why use noinline?
items: When you need to store the lambda in a variable — inlined lambdas can't be stored as objects.
When you need to pass the lambda to a non-inline function.
When you want only some lambdas inlined for performance.

sections.heading: Example

questions.question: What is crossinline in Kotlin?
questions.title: What is crossinline in Kotlin?

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: crossinline is used to prevent non-local returns in a lambda passed to an inline function, when that lambda is called from a different execution context (e.g., inside another lambda or Runnable).

sections.heading: Why it exists
items: Inline lambdas allow non-local returns (return from the outer function).
But if the lambda is passed to another scope (like a thread or callback), non-local return is unsafe.
crossinline enforces that the lambda cannot use return.

sections.heading: Example

questions.question: What are higher-order functions in Kotlin?
questions.title: What are higher-order functions in Kotlin?

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: A higher-order function is a function that takes another function as a parameter, returns a function, or both. This enables functional programming patterns in Kotlin.

sections.heading: Function as Parameter
Returning a Function
Benefits
items: Enables reusable, flexible logic.
Foundation for scope functions, collections operators (map, filter, forEach).
Reduces boilerplate by passing behavior as data.

questions.question: AssociateBy — convert List to Map in Kotlin
questions.title: AssociateBy — convert List to Map in Kotlin

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: associateBy is a Kotlin collections function that transforms a List into a Map, using a specified property of each element as the key.

sections.heading: Example
Custom key and value
Key Points
items: If duplicate keys exist, the last element wins.
Alternative: associate { it.id to it.name } for custom pairs.
toMap() works if you already have a list of Pairs.

questions.question: partition filtering function in Kotlin
questions.title: partition filtering function in Kotlin

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: partition is a Kotlin collections function that splits a collection into two lists based on a predicate — one list for elements that match, and one for elements that don't.

sections.heading: Example
sections.code:
val numbers = listOf(1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6)

val (evens, odds) = numbers.partition { it % 2 == 0 }

println(evens) // [2, 4, 6]
println(odds)  // [1, 3, 5]

sections.heading: Key Points
items: Returns a Pair<List<T>, List<T>>.
Both lists always add up to the original collection size.
Cleaner than running filter twice on the same collection.
Commonly used for separating valid/invalid data, active/inactive users, etc.

questions.question: Infix notation in Kotlin
questions.title: Infix notation in Kotlin

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: Infix notation allows you to call a function without dots or parentheses, making the code read more like natural language. A function must be marked with the infix keyword to support this.

sections.heading: Requirements for infix functions
items: Must be a member function or extension function.
Must have exactly one parameter.
Parameter must not have a default value or be vararg.

sections.heading: Example
sections.code:
infix fun Int.add(other: Int): Int = this + other

val result = 5 add 3  // Same as 5.add(3)
println(result) // 8

sections.heading: Real-world Example (Pair creation)
sections.code:
val pair = "name" to "Aasim"  // 'to' is an infix function
// Same as: "name".to("Aasim")

questions.question: What is runBlocking in Coroutines?
questions.title: What is runBlocking in Coroutines?

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: runBlocking is a coroutine builder that bridges the regular blocking world with the coroutine world. It blocks the current thread until all coroutines inside it complete.

sections.heading: Key Points
items: Blocks the calling thread — unlike launch or async.
Mainly used in main functions and unit tests.
Should NOT be used in Android UI code — it will freeze the main thread.
Creates a CoroutineScope and runs suspend functions inside it.

sections.heading: Example
In Unit Tests

questions.question: What is structured concurrency in Kotlin Coroutines?
questions.title: What is structured concurrency in Kotlin Coroutines?

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: Structured concurrency is a principle in Kotlin Coroutines that ensures coroutines are launched in a defined scope and cannot outlive that scope. It prevents coroutine leaks and makes cancellation and error propagation predictable.

sections.heading: Key Principles
items: Every coroutine belongs to a CoroutineScope.
A parent scope waits for all its child coroutines to finish before completing.
If a parent is cancelled, all children are cancelled automatically.
If a child fails, the failure propagates to the parent (unless using supervisorScope).

sections.heading: Example
Benefits
items: No coroutine leaks — all coroutines are tied to a lifecycle.
Predictable cancellation — cancel scope, cancel everything inside.
Easier error handling — exceptions bubble up naturally.

questions.question: String vs StringBuffer vs StringBuilder
questions.title: String vs StringBuffer vs StringBuilder

sections.heading: String
items: Immutable — any modification creates a new object.
Thread-safe by nature.
Best for fixed text that doesn't change.

sections.heading: StringBuffer
items: Mutable — modifications happen in-place.
Thread-safe — all methods are synchronized.
Slower than StringBuilder due to synchronization overhead.
Use when multiple threads modify the same string.

sections.heading: StringBuilder
items: Mutable — modifications happen in-place.
Not thread-safe.
Faster than StringBuffer — no synchronization.
Preferred in single-threaded scenarios for string building.

sections.heading: Kotlin Example
sections.code:
val sb = StringBuilder()
sb.append("Hello")
sb.append(" World")
println(sb.toString()) // Hello World

questions.question: val mutableList vs var immutableList — when to use which?
questions.title: val mutableList vs var immutableList — when to use which?

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: val and var control whether the reference can be reassigned, while the list type (mutable/immutable) controls whether the contents can be changed. These are independent concerns.

sections.heading: val mutableList
items: The reference cannot be reassigned, but items can be added or removed.
Use when you need a fixed reference but a growing/shrinking collection.

sections.heading: val mutableList Example
var immutableList
items: The reference can be reassigned, but you can't modify the existing list.
Use when you want to swap the entire list at once (e.g., replace full dataset).

sections.heading: var immutableList Example

questions.question: What are collections in Kotlin?
questions.title: What are collections in Kotlin?

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: Kotlin provides a rich collections framework including List, Set, and Map — each available in immutable and mutable variants.

sections.heading: Types of Collections
items: List — Ordered collection; allows duplicates. listOf() / mutableListOf().
Set — Unordered collection; no duplicates. setOf() / mutableSetOf().
Map — Key-value pairs; keys are unique. mapOf() / mutableMapOf().

sections.heading: Example
sections.code:
val list = listOf(1, 2, 3)
val set = setOf("a", "b", "a") // {a, b} — no duplicate
val map = mapOf("name" to "Aasim", "age" to 25)

sections.heading: Common Operators
items: map { } — Transform each element.
filter { } — Keep matching elements.
forEach { } — Iterate over elements.
groupBy { } — Group elements by key.
sortedBy { } — Sort by a property.

questions.question: What does ?: do in Kotlin (Elvis Operator)?
questions.title: What does ?: do in Kotlin (Elvis Operator)?

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: The Elvis operator ?: provides a default value when the left-hand expression is null. It is a concise alternative to an if-else null check.

sections.heading: Example
sections.code:
val name: String? = null
val displayName = name ?: "Guest"
println(displayName) // Guest

sections.heading: Equivalent if-else
sections.code: val displayName = if (name != null) name else "Guest"

sections.heading: Used with return or throw

questions.question: Timeouts in Kotlin Coroutines
questions.title: Timeouts in Kotlin Coroutines

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: Kotlin Coroutines provide withTimeout and withTimeoutOrNull to cancel a coroutine block if it doesn't complete within a specified duration.

sections.heading: withTimeout — throws exception on timeout
withTimeoutOrNull — returns null on timeout
Key Points
items: withTimeout throws TimeoutCancellationException.
withTimeoutOrNull is safer — returns null instead of throwing.
Duration is in milliseconds.
Prefer withTimeoutOrNull when timeout is an expected scenario.

questions.question: What is a Job in Coroutines?
questions.title: What is a Job in Coroutines?

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: A Job is a handle to a coroutine's lifecycle. It allows you to manage the coroutine — check its state, wait for it to finish, or cancel it.

sections.heading: Key States of a Job
items: Active — Coroutine is running.
Completing — Coroutine is finishing (children still running).
Completed — Finished successfully.
Cancelling — Being cancelled.
Cancelled — Fully cancelled.

sections.heading: Example
sections.code:
val job = CoroutineScope(Dispatchers.IO).launch {
    doWork()
}

job.join()   // Wait for completion
job.cancel() // Cancel the coroutine
println(job.isActive)    // false
println(job.isCancelled) // true

sections.heading: SupervisorJob
items: A special Job where child failures don't cancel siblings or the parent.
Used in viewModelScope and supervisorScope.

questions.question: Coroutines job.cancel() vs scope.cancel()
questions.title: Coroutines job.cancel() vs scope.cancel()

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: Both cancel coroutines, but at different levels of scope — job.cancel() targets one specific coroutine, while scope.cancel() cancels everything inside the scope.

sections.heading: job.cancel()
items: Cancels only the specific coroutine (and its children).
The parent scope remains active — can launch new coroutines.
Use to stop a single ongoing task.

sections.heading: job.cancel() Example
scope.cancel()
items: Cancels all coroutines in the scope.
The scope itself becomes inactive — cannot launch new coroutines.
Use when the entire lifecycle ends (e.g., Activity destroyed).

sections.heading: scope.cancel() Example
sections.code:
scope.cancel() // Cancels ALL coroutines in the scope
// scope is now dead — scope.launch {} will throw

questions.question: What happens if exception thrown inside async but await() is never called?
questions.title: What happens if exception thrown inside async but await() is never called?

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: If an exception is thrown inside an async block and await() is never called, the exception is silently ignored by default. This is a common pitfall when using async.

sections.heading: Example — Exception is lost
Correct usage — catch via await()
Key Takeaway
items: Exceptions in async are deferred until await() is called.
Always call await() inside a try-catch.
CoroutineExceptionHandler only works with launch, not async.
Use launch when you don't need a result — exceptions propagate immediately.

questions.question: How to implement debounce using Coroutines?
questions.title: How to implement debounce using Coroutines?

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: Debounce delays execution until a specified time has passed since the last trigger. In coroutines, this is typically done using Flow with the debounce operator.

sections.heading: Using Flow debounce (recommended)
Manual debounce with Job
Key Points
items: Flow debounce is cleaner and declarative.
Manual Job approach works without Flow.
Common use: search input, button click deduplication.

questions.question: How to run two coroutines in series and parallel?
questions.title: How to run two coroutines in series and parallel?

sections.heading: Series — one after another
Parallel — both at the same time
Key Points
items: Series: call suspend functions sequentially — each awaits completion before next.
Parallel: use async {} for each task, then await() all results.
Parallel is faster when tasks are independent.
If one async fails, the other is cancelled (use supervisorScope to prevent that).

questions.question: yield in Kotlin Coroutines
questions.title: yield in Kotlin Coroutines

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: yield is a suspend function in Kotlin Coroutines that pauses the current coroutine and gives other coroutines a chance to run on the same thread. It is used for cooperative multitasking.

sections.heading: Example
Key Points
items: Allows cooperative scheduling — useful with single-threaded dispatchers.
Also checks for cancellation — useful in CPU-intensive loops.
Similar to Thread.yield() but for coroutines.
Use in long-running loops to prevent starving other coroutines.

questions.question: stateIn vs shareIn in Kotlin Flow
questions.title: stateIn vs shareIn in Kotlin Flow

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: Both stateIn and shareIn convert a cold Flow into a hot Flow, but they serve different purposes based on whether you need to hold state or simply share emissions.

sections.heading: stateIn
items: Converts a cold Flow into a StateFlow.
Always holds the latest value.
New collectors receive the current value immediately.
Use for UI state that must always have a value.

sections.heading: stateIn Example
sections.code:
val uiState: StateFlow<UiState> = repository.getData()
    .stateIn(
        scope = viewModelScope,
        started = SharingStarted.WhileSubscribed(5000),
        initialValue = UiState.Loading
    )

sections.heading: shareIn
items: Converts a cold Flow into a SharedFlow.
Does not hold state by default (replay = 0).
Can replay N emissions to new collectors.
Use for events, one-shot signals, or broadcast streams.

sections.heading: shareIn Example
sections.code:
val events: SharedFlow<Event> = eventFlow
    .shareIn(
        scope = viewModelScope,
        started = SharingStarted.Lazily,
        replay = 1
    )

questions.question: flatMapConcat, flatMapMerge, and flatMapLatest in Kotlin Flow
questions.title: flatMapConcat, flatMapMerge, and flatMapLatest in Kotlin Flow

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: All three operators transform each emission into a new Flow, but differ in how they handle concurrent inner flows.

sections.heading: flatMapConcat
items: Processes inner flows sequentially — waits for each to complete before starting the next.
Preserves order. Best when tasks must run in sequence.

sections.heading: flatMapConcat Example
sections.code:
flow { emit(1); emit(2) }
    .flatMapConcat { fetchDetails(it) } // 1 finishes, then 2 starts

sections.heading: flatMapMerge
items: Processes inner flows concurrently — all start immediately.
Does not preserve order. Best for independent parallel tasks.

sections.heading: flatMapMerge Example
sections.code:
flow { emit(1); emit(2) }
    .flatMapMerge { fetchDetails(it) } // Both run at the same time

sections.heading: flatMapLatest
items: Cancels the previous inner flow when a new emission arrives.
Only the latest inner flow runs. Best for search/autocomplete.

sections.heading: flatMapLatest Example
sections.code:
searchQuery
    .flatMapLatest { query -> searchApi(query) } // Previous search cancelled on new input

questions.question: collect vs collectLatest in Kotlin Flow
questions.title: collect vs collectLatest in Kotlin Flow

sections.heading: collect
items: Collects every emission — waits for the block to finish before processing the next.
Emissions can queue up if the block is slow.
Use when every value must be processed.

sections.heading: collect Example
collectLatest
items: Cancels processing of the previous emission when a new one arrives.
Only the latest emission is fully processed.
Use for UI updates where only the freshest data matters.

sections.heading: collectLatest Example
sections.code:
flow.collectLatest { value ->
    delay(300)       // If new value arrives, this is cancelled
    updateUI(value)  // Only runs for the latest value
}

questions.question: What is a CoroutineScope?
questions.title: What is a CoroutineScope?

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: CoroutineScope defines the lifecycle boundary for coroutines. Any coroutine launched within a scope is tied to that scope — when the scope is cancelled, all its coroutines are cancelled.

sections.heading: Key Points
items: Every coroutine must be launched within a scope.
Scope provides a CoroutineContext (including the Job and Dispatcher).
Cancelling the scope cancels all child coroutines automatically.

sections.heading: Custom Scope Example
Android Built-in Scopes
items: viewModelScope — Tied to ViewModel lifecycle.
lifecycleScope — Tied to Activity/Fragment lifecycle.
GlobalScope — App-wide, avoid unless absolutely necessary.

questions.question: What is CoroutineContext in Kotlin?
questions.title: What is CoroutineContext in Kotlin?

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: CoroutineContext is a set of elements that define the behavior of a coroutine. It carries information like the dispatcher, job, and exception handler.

sections.heading: Key Elements of CoroutineContext
items: Job — Manages the lifecycle and cancellation of the coroutine.
CoroutineDispatcher — Determines which thread the coroutine runs on.
CoroutineName — Optional name for debugging.
CoroutineExceptionHandler — Handles uncaught exceptions.

sections.heading: Example
Context vs Scope
sections.content: CoroutineContext describes what and how a coroutine runs. CoroutineScope is a container that holds a context and is used to launch coroutines.

questions.question: Dispatchers in Kotlin Coroutines
questions.title: Dispatchers in Kotlin Coroutines

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: Dispatchers determine which thread or thread pool a coroutine runs on. Choosing the right dispatcher is critical for performance and avoiding ANR.

sections.heading: Available Dispatchers
items: Dispatchers.Main — Runs on the main/UI thread. Use for UI updates.
Dispatchers.IO — Optimized for I/O operations (network, database, file). Large thread pool.
Dispatchers.Default — Optimized for CPU-intensive work (sorting, parsing). Thread pool = number of CPU cores.
Dispatchers.Unconfined — Starts on the current thread, resumes on whichever thread the suspension resumes on. Rarely used.

sections.heading: Example

questions.question: coroutineScope vs supervisorScope
questions.title: coroutineScope vs supervisorScope

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: Both create a new scope for launching child coroutines, but they differ in how they handle failures from children.

sections.heading: coroutineScope
items: If any child coroutine fails, all other children are cancelled.
The exception propagates to the parent.
Use when all tasks must succeed together.

sections.heading: coroutineScope Example
supervisorScope
items: If a child fails, siblings continue running.
Exception is isolated to the failed child.
Use when children are independent and one failure shouldn't stop others.

sections.heading: supervisorScope Example

questions.question: lifecycleScope, viewModelScope, and GlobalScope — differences
questions.title: lifecycleScope, viewModelScope, and GlobalScope — differences

sections.heading: viewModelScope
items: Tied to the ViewModel's lifecycle.
Automatically cancelled when ViewModel is cleared.
Best for coroutines that should live as long as the ViewModel.

sections.heading: lifecycleScope
items: Tied to the Activity or Fragment lifecycle.
Cancelled when the lifecycle owner is destroyed.
Best for UI-related coroutines in Activities/Fragments.

sections.heading: GlobalScope
items: Lives for the entire app lifetime — not tied to any lifecycle.
Can cause memory leaks if not carefully managed.
Avoid unless you truly need an app-wide, long-running task.

sections.heading: Example

questions.question: suspendCoroutine and suspendCancellableCoroutine
questions.title: suspendCoroutine and suspendCancellableCoroutine

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: Both are used to wrap callback-based APIs into suspend functions, converting callbacks to coroutines.

sections.heading: suspendCoroutine
items: Suspends the coroutine until resume() or resumeWithException() is called.
Does NOT handle cancellation — coroutine stays suspended even if cancelled.
Use for simple, non-cancellable one-shot callbacks.

sections.heading: suspendCoroutine Example
suspendCancellableCoroutine
items: Supports cancellation — the coroutine can be cancelled while suspended.
Provides invokeOnCancellation to clean up resources when cancelled.
Preferred in most real-world cases.

sections.heading: suspendCancellableCoroutine Example

questions.question: Callback to Coroutines conversion in Kotlin
questions.title: Callback to Coroutines conversion in Kotlin

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: Legacy APIs use callbacks. Kotlin allows you to wrap these in suspend functions using suspendCancellableCoroutine, making them usable in coroutine code.

sections.heading: Callback-based API
Wrapped as a suspend function
Now usable in coroutines

questions.question: Exception handling in Kotlin Coroutines
questions.title: Exception handling in Kotlin Coroutines

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: Exception handling in coroutines depends on whether you use launch or async, and whether you use a coroutineScope or supervisorScope.

sections.heading: launch — exceptions propagate immediately
items: Use try-catch inside the launch block, or use CoroutineExceptionHandler.

sections.heading: launch with CoroutineExceptionHandler
async — exceptions deferred to await()
items: Wrap await() in try-catch to handle exceptions.

sections.heading: async with try-catch
supervisorScope — isolate failures
items: Children fail independently — handle each child's exception separately.

questions.question: withContext and when to use it
questions.title: withContext and when to use it

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: withContext is a suspend function that switches the coroutine to a different dispatcher for a block of code, then switches back automatically. Unlike async, it does not create a new coroutine — it runs inline.

sections.heading: Example
When to use withContext
items: Switching from Main to IO for network/database operations.
Switching back to Main to update UI after background work.
Preferred over async-await for single tasks — no new coroutine overhead.
Do NOT use withContext for parallel tasks — use async instead.

sections.heading: withContext vs async
items: withContext — sequential, no new coroutine, returns result directly.
async — parallel, creates Deferred, requires await().

questions.question: What is a Flow in Kotlin and how is it related to coroutines?
questions.title: What is a Flow in Kotlin and how is it related to coroutines?

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: Flow is a cold asynchronous data stream in Kotlin Coroutines that can emit multiple values over time, unlike suspend functions which return a single value.

sections.heading: Key Characteristics
items: Cold — the flow body runs only when collected.
Can emit multiple values sequentially.
Built on coroutines — uses suspend functions internally.
Supports operators like map, filter, zip, debounce, etc.

sections.heading: Basic Example
Relation to Coroutines
items: Flows must be collected inside a coroutine.
Flow operators are all suspend-aware.
flowOn {} changes the dispatcher of the upstream flow.

questions.question: Cold Flow vs Hot Flow
questions.title: Cold Flow vs Hot Flow

sections.heading: Cold Flow
items: Does NOT emit until a collector subscribes.
Each collector gets its own independent stream.
The flow body runs fresh for each collector.
Created with flow {} builder. Example: database query flow.

sections.heading: Cold Flow Example
Hot Flow
items: Emits regardless of whether collectors are subscribed.
All collectors share the same stream.
Examples: StateFlow, SharedFlow.
Common for UI state, events, sensor data.

sections.heading: Hot Flow Example
sections.code:
val hotFlow = MutableStateFlow(0) // Always active
hotFlow.value = 1 // Emits even without collector

questions.question: StateFlow and SharedFlow in Kotlin
questions.title: StateFlow and SharedFlow in Kotlin

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: Both are hot flows, but they are designed for different purposes.

sections.heading: StateFlow
items: Always holds and replays the latest value.
Requires an initial value.
Only emits when the value actually changes.
Best for UI state (replaces LiveData in many cases).

sections.heading: StateFlow Example
sections.code:
val uiState = MutableStateFlow<UiState>(UiState.Loading)
uiState.value = UiState.Success(data)

sections.heading: SharedFlow
items: Does not hold state by default (replay = 0).
Emits every event, including repeated values.
Configurable replay and buffer.
Best for one-time events like navigation, snackbar, dialog triggers.

sections.heading: SharedFlow Example
sections.code:
val events = MutableSharedFlow<UiEvent>()
events.emit(UiEvent.ShowToast("Saved!"))

questions.question: callbackFlow — Callback to Flow API in Kotlin
questions.title: callbackFlow — Callback to Flow API in Kotlin

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: callbackFlow is a Flow builder used to convert callback-based APIs (like listeners or event streams) into a Flow. Unlike the regular flow builder, it is safe to emit from different threads and supports cancellation.

sections.heading: Example — Location Listener as Flow
Key Points
items: trySend() — Emits a value (non-suspend, safe for callbacks).
awaitClose {} — Called when the flow is cancelled or no longer collected. Use to unregister listeners.
Supports backpressure via buffer configuration.

questions.question: Exception handling in Kotlin Flow
questions.title: Exception handling in Kotlin Flow

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: Exceptions in Flow can be handled using try-catch around collect, or using the catch operator in the flow pipeline.

sections.heading: Using catch operator (recommended)
sections.code:
flow {
    emit(1)
    throw RuntimeException("Error!")
    emit(2) // Never reached
}
.catch { e -> emit(-1) } // Catches upstream exceptions
.collect { println(it) } // Prints: 1, -1

sections.heading: Using try-catch around collect
Key Points
items: catch only catches upstream exceptions — not those thrown inside collect.
catch can emit fallback values.
retry and retryWhen operators allow retrying on failure.
Exceptions in collect {} must be caught with try-catch.

questions.question: SAM Conversion in Kotlin
questions.title: SAM Conversion in Kotlin

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: SAM stands for Single Abstract Method. SAM conversion allows you to use a lambda expression in place of a functional interface (an interface with one abstract method). This is a feature for Java interoperability and, since Kotlin 1.4, for Kotlin functional interfaces.

sections.heading: Java SAM (Runnable example)
Kotlin fun interface (SAM for Kotlin)
Key Points
items: Works automatically with Java functional interfaces.
For Kotlin, declare with fun interface to enable SAM conversion.
Reduces boilerplate of anonymous object declarations.

questions.question: Difference between * and Any in Kotlin generics
questions.title: Difference between * and Any in Kotlin generics

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: Both * (star projection) and Any can represent an unknown type in generics, but they have different semantics.

sections.heading: Any
items: The root of the Kotlin class hierarchy — every class is a subtype of Any.
List<Any> means a list that can hold items of any specific type.
You can add elements to a MutableList<Any>.
Loses type safety — you accept items without knowing their actual type.

sections.heading: Any Example
sections.code:
val list: MutableList<Any> = mutableListOf()
list.add("Hello")  // Allowed
list.add(42)       // Allowed

sections.heading: * (Star Projection)
items: Means the type is unknown but fixed — you don't know what type it is.
List<*> = a list of some specific type, but you don't know which.
You can only read from it (as Any?), never write to it safely.
Safer than Any for read-only use cases.

sections.heading: Star Projection Example

questions.question: What is The Billion Dollar Mistake (null references)?
questions.title: What is The Billion Dollar Mistake (null references)?

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: The Billion Dollar Mistake refers to the invention of null references by Tony Hoare in 1965, which he later called his greatest mistake. Null references have caused countless crashes, bugs, and security vulnerabilities across decades of software.

sections.heading: Why it's a problem
items: Any reference can be null unexpectedly — leads to NullPointerException (NPE) at runtime.
Java and many languages allow null for any reference type without compile-time checks.
NPEs are the most common cause of Android app crashes.

sections.heading: How Kotlin solves it
items: Types are non-nullable by default — String cannot hold null.
Nullable types are explicitly declared with ? — String? can hold null.
Null-safety checks are enforced at compile time.
Tools: safe call ?., elvis operator ?:, not-null assertion !!.

sections.heading: Example
sections.code:
val name: String = null    // Compile error
val name2: String? = null  // Explicitly nullable
val length = name2?.length ?: 0 // Safe

questions.question: How to implement Builder pattern in Kotlin?
questions.title: How to implement Builder pattern in Kotlin?

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: Kotlin reduces the need for the Builder pattern because of default parameters and named arguments. However, you can implement it using an inner class or using the apply scope function.

sections.heading: Kotlin-idiomatic Builder using apply
Classic Builder using inner class

questions.question: When to use and when NOT to use an inline function in Kotlin?
questions.title: When to use and when NOT to use an inline function in Kotlin?

sections.heading: When to USE inline
items: Higher-order functions with lambda parameters — avoids lambda object allocation.
When you need reified generics (type access at runtime).
Small utility functions called frequently — removes call overhead.
When you want to allow non-local returns from lambdas.

sections.heading: Good use of inline
When NOT to use inline
items: Functions with no lambda parameters — no performance benefit.
Large function bodies — inlining copies code everywhere, increasing bytecode size.
Recursive functions — compiler does not allow inlining recursion.
When the lambda is stored or passed to another function (use noinline instead).

questions.question: How are Kotlin coroutines better than RxKotlin/RxJava?
questions.title: How are Kotlin coroutines better than RxKotlin/RxJava?

sections.heading: Coroutines Advantages
items: Simpler, more readable code — sequential style instead of operator chains.
Built into Kotlin — no third-party library needed.
Lighter weight — coroutines are not threads; thousands can run concurrently.
Structured concurrency — automatic lifecycle management.
Less boilerplate — no need to manage subscriptions manually.
Easier error handling — standard try-catch works.
Better IDE and tooling support.

sections.heading: Where RxJava still shines
items: Mature ecosystem with a wide range of operators.
Better for complex reactive pipelines with many transformations.
Useful in Java-only projects.

sections.heading: Coroutines vs RxJava comparison

questions.question: Difference between launch/join and async/await in Kotlin Coroutines
questions.title: Difference between launch/join and async/await in Kotlin Coroutines

sections.heading: launch + join
items: launch returns a Job — no result.
join() suspends the current coroutine until the launched coroutine finishes.
Use for fire-and-forget tasks where you only care about completion, not return value.

sections.heading: launch + join Example
sections.code:
val job = launch { doWork() }
job.join() // Wait for doWork to finish, no result

sections.heading: async + await
items: async returns a Deferred<T> — a future result.
await() suspends until the result is available and returns it.
Use when you need a result from the coroutine, or for parallel computation.

sections.heading: async + await Example
sections.code: val result: String = async { computeResult() }.await()

sections.heading: Summary
items: launch/join — run task and wait; no return value.
async/await — run task and get return value; enables parallel results.

questions.question: What is the difference between Java field and Kotlin property?
questions.title: What is the difference between Java field and Kotlin property?

sections.heading: Java Field
items: A raw variable in a class — just stores a value.
No built-in getter/setter unless you write them manually.
Access is direct unless encapsulated.

sections.heading: Java Field
Kotlin Property
items: A higher-level concept that combines a field, getter, and setter.
Kotlin auto-generates getter (and setter for var) behind the scenes.
You can customize getters/setters with custom logic.
Backed by a backing field accessed via the field keyword.

sections.heading: Kotlin Property

questions.question: What is an interface vs an abstract class in Kotlin?
questions.title: What is an interface vs an abstract class in Kotlin?

sections.heading: Interface
items: Cannot hold state (no backing fields, but can have properties).
A class can implement multiple interfaces.
Methods can have default implementations.
Best for defining a contract or capability.

sections.heading: Interface Example
Abstract Class
items: Can hold state and constructors.
A class can extend only one abstract class.
Can have both abstract and concrete methods.
Best for sharing common behavior across related classes.

sections.heading: Abstract Class Example

questions.question: What are Object expressions in Kotlin and when to use them?
questions.title: What are Object expressions in Kotlin and when to use them?

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: Object expressions create anonymous objects on the fly — similar to Java's anonymous class instances. They are used when you need a one-off implementation of an interface or class without creating a named class.

sections.heading: Example — Anonymous interface implementation
When to Use
items: Implementing an interface for a one-time use.
Overriding behavior of a class temporarily.
Capturing local variables inside the object.
When SAM conversion is not applicable (multiple methods to implement).

questions.question: How would you override the default getter for a Kotlin data class?
questions.title: How would you override the default getter for a Kotlin data class?

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: You cannot directly override a getter for a primary constructor property in a data class in the standard way. The workaround is to use a private backing property and expose a computed property.

sections.heading: Problem
sections.code:
data class Test(val value: Int)
// How to return 0 if value is negative?

sections.heading: Solution — Backing property
Key Points
items: Primary constructor properties can't have custom getters directly.
Use a private val _value in the constructor and expose a public property with a custom getter.
copy() will use _value — be aware of this when using data class copy.

questions.question: What is a Kotlin backing field used for?
questions.title: What is a Kotlin backing field used for?

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: A backing field is the actual storage field generated by Kotlin for a property. It is accessed inside custom getters and setters using the keyword field. Without it, calling the property inside its own getter/setter would cause infinite recursion.

sections.heading: Why backing field is needed
sections.code:
var name: String = ""
    get() = field.uppercase()  // 'field' refers to the stored value
    set(value) { field = value.trim() } // Without 'field', this would recurse infinitely

sections.heading: Without backing field (computed property)
sections.code:
val displayName: String
    get() = "$firstName $lastName" // No backing field — computed each time

sections.heading: Key Points
items: Only available inside get() and set().
Generated automatically when a property has a default getter/setter or uses field.
Not generated for computed properties (those that only use get()).

questions.question: How to create an instance of an anonymous class of an abstract class in Kotlin?
questions.title: How to create an instance of an anonymous class of an abstract class in Kotlin?

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: In Kotlin, you use object expressions to create an anonymous instance of an abstract class, similar to Java's anonymous class syntax.

sections.heading: Example
Key Points
items: Use the object : ClassName() {} syntax.
Implement all abstract members inside the block.
Can also extend an interface or a concrete class.
The object is anonymous — it has no reusable class name.

questions.question: How to create an empty constructor for a data class in Kotlin?
questions.title: How to create an empty constructor for a data class in Kotlin?

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: Data classes require all primary constructor parameters to be provided. To allow instantiation without arguments, give all parameters default values.

sections.heading: Solution — Default Values
sections.code:
data class User(
    val name: String = "",
    val age: Int = 0
)

val emptyUser = User()         // No-arg constructor
val namedUser = User("Aasim") // Partial constructor

sections.heading: Why this is needed
items: Some libraries (Gson, Room, Firebase) require a no-argument constructor to deserialize objects.
Add @JvmOverloads if calling from Java.
For Room, the recommended approach is to use @ColumnInfo with default values.

questions.question: How would you create a singleton with a parameter in Kotlin?
questions.title: How would you create a singleton with a parameter in Kotlin?

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: Kotlin's object keyword creates a parameterless singleton. For a singleton that requires initialization parameters, you need a companion object with a manually managed instance.

sections.heading: Solution
Key Points
items: @Volatile ensures instance visibility across threads.
Double-checked locking with synchronized ensures thread safety.
The constructor is private to prevent direct instantiation.

questions.question: Why is there no static keyword in Kotlin?
questions.title: Why is there no static keyword in Kotlin?

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: Kotlin deliberately removed the static keyword to promote better object-oriented design. Instead, it offers cleaner, more explicit alternatives.

sections.heading: Replacements for static in Kotlin
items: companion object — class-level members scoped inside the class.
Top-level functions and properties — declared outside any class, accessible everywhere.
object declarations — named singletons that serve the same role as static utility classes.

sections.heading: companion object
Top-level function
Why Kotlin did this
sections.content: Static members create hidden global state and make testing harder. companion objects and top-level functions are more explicit, testable, and align with Kotlin's philosophy of clarity over convention.

questions.question: Inline classes vs type aliases — what is the difference?
questions.title: Inline classes vs type aliases — what is the difference?

sections.heading: Type Alias (typealias)
items: Just gives a new name to an existing type — no new type is created.
Both the alias and the original type are fully interchangeable.
No runtime overhead. No type safety improvement.
Useful for shortening complex types like Map<String, List<Int>>.

sections.heading: Type Alias Example
sections.code:
typealias UserId = String

fun greet(id: UserId) = println(id)
greet("abc") // String and UserId are the same type

sections.heading: Inline Class (@JvmInline value class)
items: Creates a truly distinct new type wrapping one value.
Provides type safety — UserId and String are NOT interchangeable.
No runtime object overhead — value is inlined at compile time.
Can have methods, properties, and implement interfaces.

sections.heading: Inline Class Example

questions.question: Coroutine Scope vs Coroutine Context — how are they different?
questions.title: Coroutine Scope vs Coroutine Context — how are they different?

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: These two concepts are closely related but serve different purposes in the coroutine system.

sections.heading: CoroutineContext
items: A set of elements defining HOW and WHERE a coroutine runs.
Contains: Job, Dispatcher, CoroutineName, ExceptionHandler.
Think of it as the configuration of a coroutine.

sections.heading: CoroutineContext Example
sections.code: val context: CoroutineContext = Dispatchers.IO + Job() + CoroutineName("Worker")

sections.heading: CoroutineScope
items: A container that holds a CoroutineContext.
Used to LAUNCH coroutines — defines their lifecycle boundary.
When the scope is cancelled, all coroutines launched within it are cancelled.
Think of it as the owner/manager of coroutines.

sections.heading: CoroutineScope Example
sections.code:
val scope = CoroutineScope(Dispatchers.IO + Job())
scope.launch { doWork() } // Coroutine launched within scope
scope.cancel()            // Cancels all coroutines in scope

sections.heading: Summary
sections.content: Context = what configuration to use. Scope = the boundary that manages coroutine lifecycles using that context.
